subject: ap-chemistry
title: HCl Gas Behavior, Ion-Dipole Forces & Acid Strength
stimulus: HCl is a molecular gas as a pure substance but acts as an acid in aqueous solution.

parts.prompt:
A sample of HCl(g) is stored in a rigid 6.00 L container at 7.45 atm and 296 K.

(i) Calculate the number of moles of HCl(g) in the container.

(ii) The rigid 6.00 L container of HCl(g) is cooled to a temperature of 271 K. Calculate the new pressure, in atm, of the HCl(g).
parts.scoring_notes: (i) $n = PV/RT = (7.45 \times 6.00) / (0.08206 \times 296) = 44.7 / 24.29 = 1.84$ mol. (ii) Rigid container: $V$ constant. By Gay-Lussac's Law: $P_2 = P_1 \times T_2/T_1 = 7.45 \times (271/296) = 7.45 \times 0.9155 = 6.82$ atm.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated moles of HCl(g): 1 point
wrong_examples: Using temperature in Celsius (296°C) instead of Kelvin — ideal gas law requires T in Kelvin.
Rearranging as $n = RT/(PV)$ — the ideal gas law is $PV = nRT$, so $n = PV/(RT)$.
common_traps: Ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$; solve for $n = PV/(RT)$. T must be in Kelvin. R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated new pressure: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Rigid container: $n$ and $V$ constant. By Gay-Lussac's Law ($P \propto T$ at constant $n, V$):
$P_2 = P_1 \times \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 7.45 \times \dfrac{271}{296} = 6.82\text{ atm}$

wrong_examples: Using $P_2 = P_1 \times V_1/V_2$ (Boyle's Law) — volume is constant in a rigid container, not pressure.
Using temperature in Celsius in the ratio — must use Kelvin ratios.
common_traps: Rigid container = constant V. $P/T = \text{const}$ (Gay-Lussac's Law). $P_2 = P_1(T_2/T_1)$. Both temperatures in Kelvin.

parts.prompt: When HCl ionizes in aqueous solution, $\text{Cl}^-(aq)$ ions are formed. In the following box, draw three water molecules with proper orientation around the $\text{Cl}^-$ ion.
parts.scoring_notes: The $\text{Cl}^-$ ion is negatively charged. Water molecules should orient with their hydrogen atoms (partial positive, $\delta+$) pointing toward the $\text{Cl}^-$ ion (ion-dipole interaction). The oxygen end (partial negative, $\delta-$) should point away from $\text{Cl}^-$. Three water molecules should be shown at roughly 120° apart or in a reasonable arrangement around the ion, all with H atoms directed inward toward $\text{Cl}^-$.

parts.prompt:
The following particulate diagram represents the ionization of one of the acids in the data table. Water molecules have been omitted for clarity. Which acid ($\text{HNO}_2$, HCl, or $\text{HClO}$) is represented in the diagram? Justify your answer using the information in the table.

| Acid (HA) | Anion ($\text{A}^-$) | $K_a$ value |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{HNO}_2$ | $\text{NO}_2^-$ | $5.6 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| HCl | $\text{Cl}^-$ | $2.0 \times 10^7$ |
| HClO | $\text{ClO}^-$ | $1.6 \times 10^{-8}$ |
parts.scoring_notes: The particulate diagram shows most molecules in the un-ionized HA form with only a small fraction as ions (H⁺ and A⁻). This indicates a weak acid with low $K_a$ (low degree of ionization). $\text{HNO}_2$ has $K_a = 5.6 \times 10^{-4}$ and $\text{HClO}$ has $K_a = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$. HCl is a strong acid ($K_a = 2.0 \times 10^7$) and would be nearly 100% ionized — not consistent with mostly un-ionized molecules. $\text{HNO}_2$ is a weak acid with partial ionization, consistent with the diagram showing mostly HA. (If the diagram shows very little ionization, it could be $\text{HClO}$, but if it shows moderate ionization, it is $\text{HNO}_2$.)
